data.text/markdown: Intro to pandas
Basic Concepts

The primary data structures in pandas are implemented as two classes:
  • DataFrame, which you can imagine as a relational data table, with rows and named columns.
  • Series, which is a single column. A DataFrame contains one or more Series and a name for each Series.
The data frame is a commonly used abstraction for data manipulation. Similar implementations exist in Spark and R. One way to create a Series is to construct a Series object. For example:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='Iipu-0' random-id='6253bbd9-a2c9-5938-fc96-0f22799cdf2b'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469},{\"City name\":\"San Jose\",\"Population\":1015785},{\"City name\":\"Sacramento\",\"Population\":485199}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='2' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["integer","int64"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: But most of the time, you load an entire file into a DataFrame. The following example loads a file with California housing data. Run the following cell to load the data and create feature definitions:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='ddLi-0' random-id='a5612841-0c5c-3cb6-51ef-776b07612296'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' \"households\":6082.0,\"median_income\":15.0001,\"median_house_value\":500001.0}]"' data.text/markdown: The example above used DataFrame.describe to show interesting statistics about a DataFrame. Another useful function is DataFrame.head, which displays the first few records of a DataFrame:

data.text/markdown: Another powerful feature of pandas is graphing. For example, DataFrame.hist lets you quickly study the distribution of values in a column:

Accessing Data

You can access DataFrame data using familiar Python dict/list operations:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='vXlO-0' random-id='6dee5ea7-e0e3-d471-615e-9bff0933b1ad'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"City name\":\"San Francisco\"},{\"City name\":\"San Jose\"},{\"City name\":\"Sacramento\"}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='1' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"download"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

<marimo-ui-element object-id='hKFj-0' random-id='564c899e-6327-b650-64ac-2d39447779dc'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469},{\"City name\":\"San Jose\",\"Population\":1015785}]"' data-total-rows='2' data-total-columns='2' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["integer","int64"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: In addition, pandas provides an extremely rich API for advanced indexing and selection that is too extensive to be covered here.

Manipulating Data

You may apply Python's basic arithmetic operations to Series. For example:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='cKSM-0' random-id='e93bdf74-afc2-6615-d9a2-6380ece7edc2'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"value\":852.469},{\"value\":1015.785},{\"value\":485.199}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='1' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["value",["number","float64"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"download"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: NumPy is a popular toolkit for scientific computing. pandas Series can be used as arguments to most NumPy functions:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='xGCO-0' random-id='0c156f05-15a0-a13e-3238-428b5ec4535d'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"value\":13.655892123842273},{\"value\":13.831172270553898},{\"value\":13.092314395044138}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='1' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["value",["number","float64"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"download"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: For more complex single-column transformations, you can use Series.apply. Like the Python map function, Series.apply accepts as an argument a lambda function, which is applied to each value. The example below creates a new Series that indicates whether population is over one million:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='IBlV-0' random-id='d72f2580-07ec-88b0-4e27-396fca0b5e36'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"value\":false},{\"value\":true},{\"value\":false}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='1' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["value",["boolean","bool"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"download"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: Modifying DataFrames is also straightforward. For example, the following code adds two Series to an existing DataFrame:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='hLEr-0' random-id='05c354b7-be0c-bb3f-bb63-e5432b01e556'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469,\"Area square miles\":46.87,\"Population density\":18187.945380840625},{\"City name\":\"San Jose\",\"Population\":1015785,\"Area square miles\":176.53,\"Population density\":5754.177760154082},{\"City name\":\"Sacramento\",\"Population\":485199,\"Area square miles\":97.92,\"Population density\":4955.055147058823}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='4' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["integer","int64"]],["Area square miles",["number","float64"]],["Population density",["number","float64"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown:
Exercise #1

Modify the cities table by adding a new boolean column that is True if and only if both of the following are True: 
* The city is named after a saint.

* The city has an area greater than 50 square miles.
Note: Boolean Series are combined using the bitwise, rather than the traditional boolean, operators. For example, when performing logical and, use & instead of and. Hint: "San" in Spanish means "saint."

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='Pbnw-0' random-id='9707bd86-dd42-07bc-4236-aeed548648ea'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"New column\":false},{\"New column\":true},{\"New column\":false}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='1' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["New column",["boolean","bool"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"download"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown:
Indexes

Both Series and DataFrame objects also define an index property that assigns an identifier value to each Series item or DataFrame row. By default, at construction, pandas assigns index values that reflect the ordering of the source data. Once created, the index values are stable; that is, they do not change when data is reordered.
Call DataFrame.reindex to manually reorder the rows. For example, the following has the same effect as sorting by city name:

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='mEfs-0' random-id='09ce5eb4-bedc-5249-294a-acc246666dd0'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"\":2,\"City name\":\"Sacramento\",\"Population\":485199,\"Area square miles\":97.92,\"Population density\":4955.055147058823,\"New column\":false},{\"\":0,\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469,\"Area square miles\":46.87,\"Population density\":18187.945380840625,\"New column\":false},{\"\":1,\"City name\":\"San Jose\",\"Population\":1015785,\"Area square miles\":176.53,\"Population density\":5754.177760154082,\"New column\":true}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='5' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["integer","int64"]],["Area square miles",["number","float64"]],["Population density",["number","float64"]],["New column",["boolean","bool"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[["",["integer","int64"]]]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: Reindexing is a great way to shuffle (randomize) a DataFrame. In the example below, we take the index, which is array-like, and pass it to NumPy's random.permutation function, which shuffles its values in place. Calling reindex with this shuffled array causes the DataFrame rows to be shuffled in the same way. Try running the following cell multiple times!

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='vZXm-0' random-id='0eb33595-b39c-ba5a-d26c-bd719559cdb4'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"\":0,\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469,\"Area square miles\":46.87,\"Population density\":18187.945380840625,\"New column\":false},{\"\":2,\"City name\":\"Sacramento\",\"Population\":485199,\"Area square miles\":97.92,\"Population density\":4955.055147058823,\"New column\":false},{\"\":1,\"City name\":\"San Jose\",\"Population\":1015785,\"Area square miles\":176.53,\"Population density\":5754.177760154082,\"New column\":true}]"' data-total-rows='3' data-total-columns='5' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["integer","int64"]],["Area square miles",["number","float64"]],["Population density",["number","float64"]],["New column",["boolean","bool"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[["",["integer","int64"]]]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown:
Exercise #2

The reindex method allows index values that are not in the original DataFrame's index values. Try it and see what happens if you use such values! Why do you think this is allowed?

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='NVfb-0' random-id='87c07ce6-c368-0517-5688-46bf222be2d0'><marimo-table data-initial-value='[]' data-label='null' data-data='"[{\"\":0,\"City name\":\"San Francisco\",\"Population\":852469.0,\"Area square miles\":46.87,\"Population density\":18187.945380840625,\"New column\":false},{\"\":2,\"City name\":\"Sacramento\",\"Population\":485199.0,\"Area square miles\":97.92,\"Population density\":4955.055147058823,\"New column\":false},{\"\":4,\"City name\":NaN,\"Population\":NaN,\"Area square miles\":NaN,\"Population density\":NaN,\"New column\":NaN},{\"\":5,\"City name\":NaN,\"Population\":NaN,\"Area square miles\":NaN,\"Population density\":NaN,\"New column\":NaN}]"' data-total-rows='4' data-total-columns='5' data-max-columns='50' data-banner-text='""' data-pagination='true' data-page-size='10' data-field-types='[["City name",["string","str"]],["Population",["number","float64"]],["Area square miles",["number","float64"]],["Population density",["number","float64"]],["New column",["string","object"]]]' data-show-filters='true' data-show-download='true' data-show-column-summaries='false' data-show-data-types='true' data-show-page-size-selector='false' data-show-column-explorer='true' data-show-chart-builder='true' data-row-headers='[["",["integer","int64"]]]' data-has-stable-row-id='false' data-lazy='false' data-preload='false' data-download-file-name='"df"'></marimo-table></marimo-ui-element>

data.text/markdown: This behavior is desirable because indexes are often strings pulled from the actual data (see the pandas reindex documentation for an example in which the index values are browser names). In this case, allowing "missing" indices makes it easy to reindex using an external list, as you don't have to worry about sanitizing the input.
